replies.author: @evil1st
replies.text:
​​@Crimmando Star Wars is supposed to be a fantasy scifi. The technology wasnt supposed to be in the main focus that much and the stories played out like fairy tales of the middle ages. Jedi were knights and their lightsabers were their swords. The Sith were like evil wizards and they had their forces of archers. The look and feel was supposed to be like worn down WW2 aircraft for Jedi equipment and Nazi attire for empire officers. Star Wars is like WW2 in the future told as a fairy tale.

Andor, well actually everything Star Wars these days looks and feels like Marvel mixed with Star Trek. The force is now a super power instead of a mythical force that guides. Technology is much more common and is used for basically every plot point. The Empire is shown as an evil force that just wants control when that was never the case before. The remaining Jedi are too many in numbers because the Republic Temple on Coruscant housed almost all the Jedi in the galaxy. (Remember they were only ever sent outside of Coruscant to take on missions against the sith or for trade and in very small numbers 2 to 5). Lastly modern Star Wars is all about being inclusive to all species when originally most of them never got along and humans only really got along with other bipedal humanoids. There was war on all kinds of planets before disney turned every planet into a happy oasis.

Star Wars did a complete 180 after Lucasfilm was sold. Its no longer the same idea. Today the idea of Star Wars is superheroes with laser swords VS Adolf Hitler and his room temperature IQ minions.

replies.text: Lucky you. Its a stunning trilogy. Zahn really captures the original characters and also creates great new ones. Luke is fantastic too, we get to see him as he would become and should have been in the Disney sequel trilogy. When these were first released in the early 90s I bought the first one and was sceptical it would be any good...but I was hooked in a few pages. I then went on to buy every single Star Wars book they released as they created the Expanded Universe and it put the Star Wars name back on the map with the rest of the world outside the fanbase. The only ones I did not buy or bother with were the ones where Han Luke and Leia are getting really old, years down the line...I found the Vong books about a new alien race coming in from another galaxy a bit too depressing at times, as the newer characters are killed off all over the place and the originals are now old and past it and moaning about it all the time. Who wants to read about Han Solo with back ache and shaking hands etc? But everything else with them as they were in the originals is great. I can recommend Tatooine Ghost. A great story where Leia goes to look up the places Anakin grew up in on Tatooine. She is on a mission relating to a secret code but spends most of it coming to terms with her legacy and trying to balance what Luke has told her about himself and Vader / Anakin, and what Vader did to her when she was held on the first Death Star. Its really great and makes the prequels better as well, catching up with some characters from there as she is running around Tatooine with Han and R2 learning about Vaders and Lukes past...

replies.text: Well, you just opened a door to wonderful wider universe. This is the reason that Star Wars continued to grow outside the movies; how it became the behemoth IP that it was. If i can suggest some other ripper reads amongst the 130+ EU books i have... The Darth Bane trilogy and Darth Plagues books are a must; if you want a creepy, horror flavoured Star Wars that's well written read Crosscurrent and Riptide as well as Death Troopers and Red Harvest. Tales from Jabba's Palace and Tales of the Bounty Hunters really flesh out the galaxy - these two are what made me realise that every character you see in the movies has their own story. The Courtship of Princess Leia; The Thrawn Trilogy; The Jedi Academy Trilogy; The Dark Nest Trilogy, Legacy of the Force series and the Fate of the Jedi series. Oh, Darksaber too. My favourites with some great reading in there.

replies.author: @jjc5407
replies.text:
@ there's a real mixed bag amongst that lot.

I was not a fan of Death Trooper or Red Harvest because I don't believe Star Wars is or should be a horror franchise.

The first Bane novel, Path of Destruction, was OK if very bleak until it got to the absurd ending.  The subsequent novels veered straight into Harry Potter with its spell-casting depiction of Sith.

The Jedi Academy Trilogy annoyed me with its portrayal of Luke as an ineffective teacher, Kyp Durron being described as more innately powerful than Luke and Leia and the lack of any consequences for his actions, and Kevin J Anderson's obsession with revisiting every location seen or even mentioned in the Original Trilogy.

Dark Nest, Legacy and Fate were simply dire.  Troy Denning is by far the worst of the EU writers, constantly creating ridiculous new powers and usually prefixing every display of power with the word "Force" as if it's a video game.

Give me the X-Wing novels over any of those (except Mercy Kill, which was s belated entry and a huge disappointment).

replies.author: @frocat5163
replies.text:
@DaFIX576 
*"The thing with Marvel though was that it was a risk at first. "*
Except it absolutely wasn't. At least not for Disney. _Iron Man_ was produced by Marvel Studios and distributed by Paramount. It was filmed from March to June 2007, and released in May 2008. Disney didn't purchase Marvel Studios until August 31, 2009, more than a year after _Iron Man_ was released. Disney bought Marvel Studios knowing full well that _Iron Man_ had been a resounding success, as were the X-Men films by Fox, and the Spiderman films by Sony, and they were eager to continue that success. From 2000 through 2009, most Marvel movies released were successful.

And of course they didn't know it was going to do as well as it did over the next decade. Just like George Lucas had no idea _Star Wars_ was going to be as successful as it was.

replies.author: @frocat5163
replies.text:
@Tomas-ih1hp 
*"You just want to hate anti fascists"*
Considering so-called "anti-fascists" are more fascist than actual fascists, yes, I absolutely want to hate on them. They deserve it.

*"if characters like Padme and Leia were also modern..."*
Well, they _are_ modern, genius. They were both created less than 50 years ago. Your lack of intelligence doesn't change reality, no matter how much you wish it did.

*"...yall wouldve called them mary sue..."*
Tell us you don't have a clue what you're talking about without saying you don't have a clue what you're talking about. No one would call Leia or Padme a Mary Sue...because they aren't Mary Sues. You clearly have no idea what that term means, other than what your -anti- fascist overlords have told you it means. Allow me to educate you (even though I know attempting to educate someone like you is equivalent to convincing a flat Earther that the Earth isn't a magical dirt pizza). The term 'Mary Sue' comes from a piece of Star Trek fanfiction published in 1973 in which the author's stand-in character, named Mary Sue, was the youngest ever ensign in Starfleet history (15 and a half years old), and assigned to the USS Enterprise as bridge crew. Mary Sue was loved by all of the established characters for no other reason than she existed. She was a better pilot than Sulu. She was a better engineer than Scottie. She was a better doctor than McCoy. She was a better science officer than Spock. She never had to struggle for anything. She routinely saved the Enterprise when Kirk, Spock, Bones, Scottie, et al were completely baffled by the current problem. And when she died, she was mourned more deeply than anyone has ever been mourned by literally everyone. THAT'S a Mary Sue...and neither Leia nor Padme are anything like that. Rey, on the other hand, is *EXACTLY THAT.* She is a better Jedi than Yoda after a long weekend of Jedi training that she bailed out of halfway through. She knows the _Millennium Falcon_ better than Han Solo and Chewbacca after being onboard for about 10 minutes. She's a better pilot than Han, Luke, Wedge, Biggs, and Anakin the first time she touched the stick, despite never flying anything more complex than her speederbike. 

*"...whatever that idiotic misogynistic term is"*
It's fantastic that you admit you have no idea what you're talking about. Of course, it's pathetic that, despite acknowledging your total ignorance of the topic at hand, you still feel qualified to spew your asinine opinion here like the intellectual equivalent of a flat Earther with an IQ below that of the typical flat Earther. Well done.

*" I love how incels like you..."*
And we love how tools like you screech that anyone who dislikes a poorly written female character must be an incel. Just because you can't get any of the women you're Stanning for to touch your naughty bits doesn't mean the rest of us are equally incapable of interacting with females. Screeching "INCEL" because someone says something you don't like is almost a pathetic as your admitted ignorance of literally everything associated with Star Wars.

replies.text:
The Prequels are far from perfect, but unlike Disney's pathetic efforts they do have good elements.  Palpatine's plot to take over the galaxy is genius-level writing that doesn't get the recognition it deserves.

George gave us new worlds we hadn't seen before unlike Disney who basically gave us "Desert World that wasn't Tatooine", "Rebel (sorry Resistance) Base World that wasn't Yavin", Death Star World crossed with Hoth, Salt World that looked like Hoth too especially with all the AT-ATs (or whatever their designation was), Endor pretending to be Kamino with a great roiling ocean... and so on.

George also gave us new vehicle designs and showed an evolution towards those in the OT.  Disney went back to Tie Fighters (with tissue paper panels) and X-Wings with bizarre semi-circular turbines (how do those work?!), and Imperial Walkers and Star Destroyers that were just "bigger".

The lack of thought and originality from Disney is staggering.

replies.text:
@robertdouble559 The audience has heard the boy cry wolf about Star Wars being good again so many times over the last 10 years that they don't believe it anymore. So when something that's genuinely well made comes along, they make the perfectly logical assumption that it'll be as shitty as everything else. It's a reflexive response to the mountains of dogshit they've had to put up with for a whole decade.

Andor and Rogue One are the EXCEPTIONS. And because the exceptions are so rare, people won't risk opening up because they've been disappointed far too many times. Many have moved on simply to protect themselves emotionally.

That's not stupidity. That's not an inability to comprehend nuance. That's not a lack of understanding about storytelling. 

That's simply a case of not trusting Disney. 

And Star Wars fans have every right to lose faith because Disney is a horrific company that has little to no understanding of Star Wars. 

Even Tony Gilroy himself relies on raw writing talent, not an understanding of Star Wars lore in and of itself. Ask him about the lightsaber combat forms, any of the ancient Jedi and Sith, or how balance in the Force actually works in accordance to George Lucas and I guarantee he wouldn't have a clue what the answers are. Because he is NOT a Star Wars fan. Just a professional who happens to work on it.

But unlike the other imbeciles who work on Star Wars now, Gilroy is smart enough to know his limitations. That's why he succeeds where everybody else fails. It's not because he's knowledgeable about Star Wars. It's because he's intelligent. Andor - and Rogue One for that matter - work because they're malleable enough to fit into Star Wars by not relying on stuff that Gilroy either isn't interested in or doesn't understand. He simply sticks to what he knows.

replies.author: @victorcates9330
replies.text:
Some people described it as having an implication of coercion. Firstly, I think the issue is more that it's cartoonish. It's easily fended off and morally rigs the deck. The rebels are partisans. Partisans do gross stuff. The resistance to the germans were entirely capable of doing stuff that had civilian death tolls (if your information was faulty, it would be possible to blow up the wrong train, for instance). After the war, I think the people took revenge. I'm told, but I'm not sure, that included parading women deemed collaborators naked in the street as a form of sexual humiliation. The germans and russian soldiers definitely did weird stuff. In Israel, the resistance to the British empire included acts of terror. So if you're living in a supposedly morally sophisticated world, you can rig the deck. Whatever the rebels do, the empire are positioned as committing something next level.

If the empire is an analog for the germans, then I guess. If the empire is an analog for Pinochet, I guess. If it's meant to be a critique of power - thus implicitly a critique of the US government, then this kind of violence seems provocative. If it's just a plot device meant to move the character, then you'd get a different criticism. Invoking the spectre becomes iffy if you don't capture a real dynamic. She swats him away with the efficiency you'd swat a fly. It undercuts physical vulnerability if it's immediately resolved and she could have kicked his ass at will. Broaching something that has a gravity, they risk being roasted for being glib.

It would have been more morally interesting if the rebels had aligned themselves with a guy who tried to use the chaos as cover to take advantage. They could have called the character representing a progressive force superficially but being a piece of excrement Warvey Heinstein.

@ Alden Ehrenreich of course.  The movie's not set that many years before A New Hope to remotely convince that he's a younger version.  It's not his fault.  You'd be hard pressed to find any actor that can convince when the age gap is that small at that time of a character's life. 

It isn't like a 9-year old Jake Lloyd and Hayden Christensen or Ewan McGregor as a young Alec Guinness (and frankly the Kenobi series, in typically lazy Disney fashion, made no effort to make the former look as if he was aging into the latter).

Ehrenreich simply doesn't convince as Han Solo.  The story is also awful and it's a film that had no reason to exist.  How Han got his surname is cringe-inducing.  His meeting with Chewie is terribly handled.  The record-breaking Kessel Run is eye-rollingly bad.  It's no surprise this movie lost money at the box office.

Frankly if you want stories about Han from before A New Hope there are two novel trilogies that do a far, far better job of it.

@Clutch_JAD Let’s say Disney bought the sole rights to produce Ice Cream.

But, they changed the formula and made it out of manure and salt instead of cream and sugar.

Can you imagine how pissed off the millions of ice cream lovers would be?

They would universally cry out, “This isn’t ice cream.”

But, then that one person… who’s either 1) never tasted real ice cream or 2) likes the taste of manure and salt… stands up and asks, “Why do you millions of angry people say this isn’t ice cream?”

We’d all look at that person, with manure and salt smeared innocently across their rosy cheeks, and think to ourselves: “Are they kidding? Or, do they really like the taste of that SaltyAssCrap?”

😵‍💫

@Reedme203 People keep using this line over and over and over and over again and I don't get it. 

"it's meant to give us an escape from the harsh pain of our realities for a little bit." 

By watching fictional characters go through the same thing or worse? That's fiction for you. They need to have a conflict that makes it interesting to watch. Otherwise you could just look up funny fail or animal videos to escape for a while. In the very first movie, Luke lost both his aunt and uncle, even if said loss was what he needed to properl him on his journey. Leia is implied to have been very harshly interrogated and is forced to watch as her home planet gets obliterated with her adoptive father on it.

Or is it you only want safe stories where you know the heroes will always win? There can be inspirational themes, yes, but you will rarely get there before the characters have to be brought low at some point. 

Thinking of Star Wars as just the comfort food equivalent of entertainment is what has caused people not take it seriously since I was a kid. As merely a nerd's thing. There space for different types of stories within the universe Lucas created.

If they did this to the farm girl instead of bix it would at least serve as a character development moment to change a neutral party into a rebel, for it to happen to bix was unnecessary as she was already tortured and her friends had been murdered by the empire.
